Yeah, I feel like the main theme, what are some misconceptions that you feel like people have, either about Datagrid or the industry, that you're like, I just wish I could explain this once and everyone would have this understanding?
Any big, like, narrative bets for you this quarter?
Technical credibility, speed, safety, anyway.
Anyway.
And run through the rest.
But those, I feel like, are some of the main ones.
And then any patterns and what, like, mature users, maybe people who've been using Datagrid for a long time are doing versus – or mature users versus, like, early adopters.
Like, how do they use Datagrid differently?
So those are a couple of jumping off points.
Yeah.
Yeah, think, like, we – I mean, one of the key things that I've been kind of realizing is that the first couple use cases for AI were, I would say, search and summarize.
Those are the two main use cases that we saw AI being used for at scale.
Like, everybody can use search and summarize in some shape or form.
But now we're entering this phase where there's all these tasks that AI can execute are not difficult tasks, but they are draining tasks.
They're tasks that people actually absolutely hate doing.
And I think the tasks that people hate doing are the ones that are –
Easier to replace with AI, rather than, you know, try to replace the stuff that people love doing, right?
Yeah.
So, you know, per se, like, if you look at, you know, in our customer base, like, people deal with a lot of PDFs, a lot of files, and very often they're dealing with, like, 1,000-page PDFs.
I was just yesterday with a client, and literally during the call, he got somebody to text him and ask him a question about what was decided about this particular thing they had to install.
And he pulled his screen, and he had five documents he was sifting through, five PDF documents, and each one of them was, like, 1,700 pages.
So, finding the thing in there, and actually getting the answer to that dimension that he needed to find the answer to, is the draining task for him.
Like, it's the main thing that just sucks, you know, sucks his energy out.
Yeah.
And I think that's where people want the AI to do work for them.
And then very often you talk to people and they're like, well, can you just do the thing, right?
Like, not just find it.
there's a small – there's a gap between finding and doing that people are discovering now.
You know, summarize is not do because, like, I search so that I can do something.
But how do I actually just do something?
How do I actually ask the editor to do the thing and then get it to do it for me?

So I think that's the next phase that we're going to see of adoption of AI, at least we're seeing, is this thing of, you know, this sort of, like, natural behavior that you expect from, like, a new turn on Attach to Hate.
You know, you want to offload those things to AI, I think, to them.
Yeah, that makes perfect sense.
Yeah, go ahead.
But, you know, that narrative bet would be that people are going to replace stuff they hate doing more quickly with AI than replacing their role.
Per se.
Per
Makes perfect sense.
Like, that's the logical conclusion.

Can you just do that action for me?
It feels like the natural kind of progression of you get used to something, and then you're kind of, like, dissatisfied, you know?
You're sort of like, okay, this thing that felt miraculous at one point, 1,700 pages, you can easily find the answer for me.
But now I still have to do the manual part of, like, knitting this together in the next step that I need to take.

And the AI filtered down to 50 things that are related to electrical installation with the beginning and end dates, you know?
So it's super simple, but, like, we're also producing these.
Documents between teams, between people, between companies, especially companies that don't operate in the digital world.
They operate in the physical world.
They produce a lot of documentation like this that has to be used to communicate.
And I think AI is kind of the ultimate communication tool between people.
And it's just not being used yet.
It's like it's ready to be used, but it's not being used if you look at it.

And do you think it's not being used in part because people aren't – especially like these industries that maybe rely upon all of these different types of documents to like communicate like regulations or all these complicated processes that they just like aren't aware – you're doing a lot of like education of like what you want it to do, it currently can do.
And maybe people are just kind of – yeah, I guess my question is, is that what you're noticing, that people just aren't aware of the capabilities and you just kind of have to do a lot of education?
And then the –
It kind of sells itself?
No, I think it's the reason is because the people that are using AI today are the people that have had the time to learn it.

So Marcel was working and he had a full-time job and he would go home and he would spend until midnight or one in the morning learning AI and building these workflows so he could automate what he wished that was automated in his day-to-day.
So Marcel is an exception.
He could actually do this.
He could actually go home and then, like, try to make AI work for marketing.
There's somebody that is trying to make AI work for this thing, but the rest of the population, they have a job to do.
They can't stop to learn AI.
So the people that actually know AI today, they are the ones that work over time to perfect their skill with AI.
So there's a very small number of people.
Now, the new people that are coming into the job market, those graduated using AI.
They have a good school.
So...
Using AI, they did everything using AI.
They wrote their marriage speech using AI.
They did all this stuff using AI, so they can't work in any other way.
It's like if you come into the market today and you can't use Google Docs or Google Sheets.
That would be crazy to imagine.
But the number of people that just have never really stopped to learn a better tool is staggering, is massive.
Like a majority of the population, in fact, has not stopped to learn how to do things in a better way because they just don't have the time.
It's really that, I think.
Yeah, it's interesting.
I feel like obviously the particularities of AI are super contemporary, of course, but also I feel like none of it is actually authentic.
that's surprising because that's kind of been the same, that applies to like any technological adoption.

It's, like, they know how to open Chatspeed and answer the question, but that's not mastering AI, and then the last thing, I think, is mastering AI.
AI, like truly mastering AI.
think there's a whole thing to be said about that.
But, you know, just think about it.

There's a whole thing there.

I did that before, so it's easier for me.

So it's really tough out there because, like, companies are searching for more innovative people, and there's such severe lack of them.
So, yeah, I would definitely vouch for that statement, yeah.

Because that's almost becoming obsolete.

As a user, like, aren't you leaving a lot behind because the opportunity for you to, like, really understand, like, how to use this as an execution tool that can do a lot for you, like, the opportunity to learn that.
And do that?
Is that a unique point in time, per se?
Because there was a point in time where you could be, like, you know, inventing Amazon.
There was a point in time where that could happen.
And that point is gone and is probably never happening again.

And it's, like, how many of those opportunities exist right now that people aren't really harnessing because they stopped short or because – so there's a whole thing about this is going to be the era of persistence or, like, people that can achieve enough attention or, like, put enough attention in something for long enough.
They're going to differentiate because basically, like, anybody can do – like, there's no excuse to have poor writing right now.
You know, like, before there was no excuse to have typos in your email.
There was no excuse because you had Grammarly.
Now there's no excuse to badly communicate or write bad marketing or, you know, have – you know, there's just only bad ideas are going to be –
You know, it's going to be easy to spot the bad idea because even a bad idea can be spotted when it's badly well-written.
You know, even a bad idea well-written is going to be bad anyway.
Like a bad movie acted by the best actor, you know, like really like we are in that same thing with AI.
You wrote a bad script and you hired a best actor and you got the best director, but the script was just really bad.
Like the movie is going to be bad.
You're not going to get an Oscar for the movie.
So we're at the same point now with AI where you can have infinite amounts of good actors acting for you.
But there is no way to, you know, even if you hire the best of the best of the best and your idea is bad and you just give bad things to the AI, the output is going to be still bad.
So now is the moment where you can be like you have to have a life experience to actually interject into these things to be able to like really be unique.

think this is one thing that – I mean, part of it is, like, all the stuff we just talked about.

One that might be slightly, like, top of mind for you is what should always be true about Datagrid's agent no matter what you're building?
Like, what should consistently be true no matter what the application is?
Which I have have, put sawyer Thank
I think, I mean, you should always be trying to do, like, anticipate steps and getting things done for you, you know, with the goal that you intended.
So this user, and I posted about this a couple days ago, but this user asked the AI to file this very thing I was showing you guys into SharePoint.
And the agent actually figured out that he had to, like, select all these different parts of this document, compose a new document, and then send that document to SharePoint.

Like, that's huge, because if you had only found the things for me and said, these are the things you asked me for, then I have to select that and then put it somewhere and then save it as a PDF and then open SharePoint and upload SharePoint.
And those sound like small, fast, probably five, ten minutes, but they're draining, they're painful.
So the AI has to anticipate what the final goal, final objective is, and learn from that.
And that's what we're engineering this thing for, is, like, anticipate what the final objective is.
And write it.
Like my LinkedIn writing, I think you guys see my LinkedIn writing recently, but I'll show you how this thing works for me.
When I threw – like I was trying to write my bio, right?
And it did a bunch of different things to actually help me write my bio and make it interesting.
Like the quality between the bio I wrote and how the AI thought about like – so this is not just writing my bio.
This is like literally helping me think about what my bio could be.
But without me going and saying, can you think about this?
Like really like it came up with all these ideas.
Like why am I going to go down to like this closing reflection versus, you know, something else?
And it sharpened my bio.
My bio was like 10 times more interesting with the writing of the – like the dog fluting stuff that we did.
Like started –
Off with, like, me pasting an image and, like, hey, I want to write about this, you know?
And what do I want to write about this?
I want to write, like, an interesting LinkedIn article, right?
So, like, at the end of this, this had, like, a really good article.
It was actually posted by Miles, not by me.
And this is an insight that the AI had, right?
Like, literally, this is an insight about that image.
So I think, like, AI should work like this.
You should be able to, like, give a little bit of input, and you should be able to go, like, a few steps further.
And same thing with creating a deal in HubSpot for me.
Like, I asked it to create a deal for Sutter Health, and it created a deal in HubSpot.
But it didn't just create the deal.
It was researching, you know, Sutter Health, and it was the type of, like, revenue cycle is their use case.
It actually researched about revenue cycle.
It actually researched revenue cycle on the Athena website.
And...
And...
And on the other health companies' websites to create the deal with SutterHealth that described the revenue cycle solution that we were going to implement for them.
Like, I didn't actually explain.
I just say create a deal for SutterHealth worth $50K, Tom Pauly is the owner, and a use case in SutterHealth.
I didn't explain what that specific use case was.
And the final entry in HubSpot contained all of the specifics around the deal.
So I think, like, I'm getting used to work like this.
It's really, like, you know, like, when I create the deal, it creates an opportunity.
When I create slides, I asked it to create a slideshow for me.
It created 12 slides for me that were, like, really good.
It's added to slides, to Google Slides, and so on.
So it just has to work a little bit, like, ahead of the user and think more so that the final output is, like, where the user is going, not just exactly what they're going to see.
Yeah.
So when you're – and I know that we're at that time, so I'm trying not to go over too much.
But so when you're saying, like, it's not so much, like, necessarily a misconception that we're
Talking about it's like you kind of educating the AI user to think in this way where it's like you can ask – your prompts don't even have to be excellent.
Like it can be relatively like do X because of Y and it can take you to that end point and it's just letting people kind of imagine like a better reality than maybe what they're used to with AI.
Is that kind of the right way of putting it?
Yeah, and also like don't use it as an autocomplete, you know, try to use it as a colleague.
Like that's the way I'm using it.
Like, hey, create a deal for better health, you know.
Like I was asking it, I'm going to have a meeting today with a few people, just, you know, run my calendar by me.
And he came back and said, you're meeting Chris Young.
This is the part that Chris Young is working on.

And Chris Young had an exit and he worked at this company.
I literally showed it to Chris Young.
And he was like, holy .
That's awesome.

This is  amazing.
So, like, I think we have this working.
A lot of people don't because, like, Gemini doesn't work like this.
ChatGPG doesn't work like this.
But we are, like, pioneering these workflows in terms where we just say, hey, like, I'm going to meet, like, with these people.
Just tell me what's going on.
And the AI just, like, does the additional steps.
Yeah.
Rather than just being sort of, like, trying to answer you really quickly with something that is mildly useful.
Yeah, for sure.
You're to have to be a favorite of how we use AI.
It's, like, it started off as an autocomplete for a lot of people.
But I think it's becoming, like, really more like, you know, a colleague or a… Yeah.
